## Anatomy & mechanics of 4 joints of shoulder complex

### Sternoclavicular Joint
![[Pasted image 20250210005013.png|center]]
#### Structure
- **Articular Surface**
	Between Clavicle & Manubrium, form Saddle joint, 2 pairs [[Convex-Concave principle|convex-concave]] surfaces sitting perpendicular
	Allowing 2 planes of movement
![[Pasted image 20250126215129.png|center]]
- **Ligaments**
	- Anterior + Posterior sternoclavicular joint ligaments (SCL)
	- Interclavicular ligament (ICL)
	- Costoclavicular ligament (CCL)

Different movement will cause ligaments to be taut or slack
(refer to pic)
Rule of thumb:
Move towards: slack e.g. PSL slack during elevation
Move away: taut e.g. CCL taut during elevation
![[Sternoclavicular Joint|center|500]]

**Joint Capsule**
	Anterior Disc

**Muscles**
	1. Sternocleidomastoid
	2. Sternothyroid
	3. Sternohyoid
	4. Subclavius

#### Movement

Main driver of movement is humerus

| Elevation/Depression                             <br>a. Convex on concave movement                                                                                  | Anterior View![[Pasted image 20250126214916.png]] |
| :------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------- |
| Protraction/retraction<br>a. Concave on convex movement                                                                                                             | ![[Pasted image 20250126215006.png\|center]]      |
| Posterior rotation<br><br>a. Involuntary movement as a result of shoulder abduction<br>b. Not a physiological movement of the surfaces - caused by ligament tension | ![[Pasted image 20250126215014.png]]              |


### Glenohumeral Joint

#### Structure:
- Scapula & Humerus
- [[types of joints|Ball & Socket]]
- Humeral Head does not fit perfectly into glenoid fossa allowing for more movement but also less stability
- Anatomical position:
	Glenoid fossa: upward rotated, articular surface project antero-laterally, aligning to scapular plane
	Humeral head: project medially, superiorly, posteriorly due to retroversion (rotating back)

- Diameters
	Longitudinal side: humeral head 1.9x glenoid fossa
	Transverse side: humeral head 2.3x glenoid fossa
	Result: 1/3 humeral head covered
![[Pasted image 20250126220332.png|center]]
#### Mobility:

**Articulating Surface**
	Not pure ball & socket
	Scapular movements provide extra mobility
	Lined with articular cartilage for lubrication of joint surface

**Joint capsule**
	Vol is 2x humeral head- increasing movement
	Synovial membrane lines inner wall of capsule - lubrication

#### Stability
1. Ligaments:
	1. Capsular ligaments
	i. Superior GH ligament
	ii. Middle GH ligament
	iii. Inferior GH ligament complex

2. Coracohumeral ligament

Heal by fibrosis, causing stiffness in body
Joint Capsule:
	Fibrous capsule strengthened by GH capsular ligaments
Glenoid Labrum
Muscles:
	Rotator cuff
	Long head biceps brachii
	Biomechanics of scapulothoracic posture


### Acromioclavicular(AC) Joint

#### Structure
- **Articular Surface**
	- Between Clavicle & Acromion
	- Plane Synovial joint allowing 3 rotational + 3 translational degrees of freedom

- **Ligaments**
	1. Superior + Inferior acromioclavicular joint ligaments
	2. Coracoclavicular ligament

- **Articular Disc**

- **Muscles**
	1. Deltoids
	2. Upper trapezius
#### Movement

Main driver of movement: Scapula
Note: due to attachments and force couple dynamics, these movements normally do not occur in isolation

|   |   |
|---|---|
|Upward/Downward rotation<br><br>Rmb: Rotate anticlockwise/clockwise (will differ from left and right scapula)|![](data:image/png;base64,)|
|Internal/External Rotation<br><br>Rmb: Curve Angle in and outwards along ribs<br><br>Note : not the same as abduction/adduction|![](data:image/png;base64,)|
|Anterior/Posterior tilting<br><br>Rmb: Curving away/towards ribs|![](data:image/png;base64,)|
### Scapulothoracic (pseudo joint)

- ==Not true joint!!!==
	
Formed by:
	- Scapula & posterior-lateral thoracic wall positioned at ribs 2-7 in anatomical position
	- Separated by muscles
	- Absence of joint capsule
	- Resting posture:
		- 10$\degree$ anterior tilt
		- 10$\degree$ upward rotation
		- 30 - 40$\degree$ internal rotation
	
- Importance + Functional advantage
	
	- Scapulothoracic upward rotation contributes to 1/3 of shoulder elevation
	- Shifts glenoid fossa upwards and antero-laterally to increase elevation
	- During abduction, allows supraspinatus and mid deltoids to preserve optimal length-tension relationship, produce efficient force
	- Preserve vol of subacromial space, avoiding impingement

### movement of scapula region

Scapula joint movements happen in tandem
Due to natural anatomy ie muscle attachment, contours of the ribs
Scapulothoracic movements result as a combination of SC and AC movements

| Scapulothoracic                 | Sternoclavicular           | Acromioclavicular              |
| ------------------------------- | -------------------------- | ------------------------------ |
| Elevation/<br>Depression        | Elevation/<br>Depression   | Anterior/<br>Posterior tilting |
| Protraction/<br>Retraction      | Protraction/<br>Retraction | Internal/<br>External Rotation |
| Upward/<br>Downward<br>Rotation | Elevation/<br>Depression   | Upward/<br>Downward Rotation   |
